question_title: What will the ATP Singles #1 ranking points be on April 27, 2026?
summary: Carlos Alcaraz is currently ATP #1 with 13,550 points and a 3,150-point lead over Sinner. Between now and April 27, he defends 1,700 points from strong 2025 March-April results: Indian Wells SF (360), Miami 2R (10), Monte Carlo title (1000), and Barcelona final (330). He withdrew from Madrid 2025 (0 to defend). His exceptional 2026 form (12-0, won AO/Doha/Dubai) gives him strong chances of replacing most defending points, but the Monte Carlo 1000-point defense is the biggest swing factor—anything less than a repeat title means net loss from that event alone. Monte Carlo simulation of 100K outcomes models tournament-by-tournament results with ~30% win probability at each Masters and realistic distributions for other rounds. The median outcome is ~13,420 points (slight decline from current), with meaningful uncertainty in both directions. Alcaraz is ~97% likely to remain #1 given his massive lead.

factors.description: Alcaraz currently at 13,550 pts, defending 1,700 pts (IW SF 360, Miami 2R 10, MC W 1000, Barcelona F 330) from March-April 2025. Net expected change slightly negative as defending strong results is hard to fully replicate.
Alcaraz is in exceptional 2026 form (12-0, won AO, Doha, Dubai). Increases probability of strong results at IW, Miami, MC, Barcelona.
Monte Carlo defense (1000 pts) is biggest risk. Any result below a repeat title means net points loss from this tournament alone.
Withdrew from Madrid 2025 (0 to defend), so Madrid 2026 early rounds (starts April 20) provide small net positive of 10-45 pts.
3,150-point lead over Sinner makes it ~97% likely Alcaraz remains #1. If Sinner were #1 instead, his points would be lower (~12,000).
Historical #1 points in late April range 7,000-13,000 in recent years, but Alcaraz's total is exceptionally high. Even with point losses, likely remains above 12,500.

reasoning:
**Forecast: ATP Singles #1 Ranking Points on April 27, 2026**

**Key findings:**
- **Current #1:** Carlos Alcaraz with 13,550 points (3,150 ahead of Sinner)
- **Defending March-April:** 1,700 total points (IW SF 360, Miami 2R 10, Monte Carlo W 1000, Barcelona F 330)
- **Madrid 2025:** Withdrew (0 to defend), so Madrid 2026 early rounds are pure upside
- **2026 form:** Exceptional (12-0, won Australian Open, Doha, Dubai)

**Distribution (from 100K Monte Carlo simulations):**

| Percentile | Points |
|-----------|--------|
| 10th | 12,480 |
| 20th | 12,800 |
| 40th | 13,230 |
| 60th | 13,595 |
| 80th | 14,055 |
| 90th | 14,395 |

The median sits near 13,420 — a slight decline from current 13,550 — because defending a Monte Carlo title (1000 pts) plus Indian Wells SF and Barcelona final is inherently difficult to replicate in full, even for a dominant player. The upside scenario (winning 2+ of these events) pushes above 14,000, while early exits at multiple tournaments could push toward 12,500.

resolution_criteria: This question resolves to the numeric value of the ranking points displayed for the ATP Singles #1 on the ATP rankings page for the week ended April 27, 2026 (or, if no rankings are published for that exact date, the closest rankings publication date immediately prior to then). The primary source is the ATP rankings page at [https://www.atptour.com/en/rankings/singles](https://www.atptour.com/en/rankings/singles)
fine_print: Use the points number shown next to the player ranked #1 on the relevant rankings date. Candidate Sources: ATP rankings archive navigation within the same page
background_info:
Ranking points at #1 reflect dominance and the distribution of tour performance over the prior 52 weeks.
